text: I have a question. What's the longest anyone has ever held their breath? Do not try this at home. For experts, this is really cool science. If you've never trained to hold your breath, like me, you could probably do a minute. But as you do, the oxygen in your body starts decreasing, and the carbon dioxide starts increasing, because you're not exhaling it. Most of us hit our limit less because of lack of oxygen, and more because the increase in buildup in the CO2 tells our brains to exhale. You have a bunch of chemical sensors near your throat that detect oxygen and CO2 levels in your blood, and eventually cause involuntary breathing movements, basically spasms that make you breathe. So experts train with specific breath holding routines that build up a high tolerance to the CO2, and minimize the amount of oxygen that your body needs to function. But they can also wildly increase the amount of oxygen they start with by breathing pure O2 beforehand instead of normal air. So the official record for normal air and no moving is 11 minutes and 35 seconds. And now with pure oxygen, if you think it's over 20 minutes, follow along. 29 minutes and three seconds. Did you get it right?
